mappings.rationaleAI: Numerical operations across number systems correspond to fundamental definitions and laws (B.1.a), theory of divisors and fractions (B.1.b), numeral systems and positional notation (B.1.c), decimal calculation (B.1.d), and logarithms (B.1.e).

Comprehensive coverage from Euclidean geometry (C.1) through non-Euclidean (C.2), projective (C.3), analytic and trigonometric (C.4), and differential geometry (C.6); covers points, lines, planes, manifolds, and symmetry.

Continuous functions, limits, differentiation, integration, and measure theory (D.1); complex analysis (D.2); differential equations (D.3); functional analysis (D.4); Fourier analysis and series (D.5).
Elementary algebra with equations and variables (B.2), linear algebra including vector spaces (B.3), and abstract algebraic structures -- groups, rings, fields, modules, category theory (B.4).

Elementary number theory on integers (E.2.a), algebraic number theory (E.2.b), analytic number theory including Riemann zeta function (E.2.c), geometric number theory (E.2.d), and probabilistic number theory (E.2.e).
Fundamental geometric object defined by Euclid's postulates in Euclidean geometry (C.1, C.1.a); linear equations in plane analytic geometry (C.4.a).
Generalization of Euclidean geometry to higher dimensions (C.1.e); projective and solid analytic geometry (C.4.d); vector and tensor analysis in three dimensions (D.7).
General topology covering topological spaces, continuity, compactness (F.1); differential topology (F.2); algebraic topology including fundamental group, homology, cohomology (F.3).
Repeated multiplication extended to various exponents connects to arithmetic calculation with decimals and roots (B.1.d); inverse operation (logarithms) is treated in B.1.e.
Origins and definitions of sets (A.1), fundamental set concepts and Cantorian set theory (A.2), and axiomatic set theory including ZFC (A.3).
Integers as the smallest ring containing natural numbers relate to fundamental definitions and laws of arithmetic (B.1.a); properties of whole numbers in elementary number theory (E.2.a).
Formal definition via Peano axioms relates to fundamental concepts of natural numbers and integers (B.1.a); set-theoretic construction connects to Cantorian set theory (A.2.b).
Groups (B.4.b), rings (B.4.d), fields (B.4.c), lattices (B.4.a), categories (B.4.e), and universal algebra (B.4.g) are the core algebraic structures.
Vector spaces (B.3.a.i), matrices (B.3.a.ii), linear transformations (B.3.a.iii), and inner products (B.3.a.v) are the core objects of linear algebra.
Natural numbers and integers (B.1.a), fractions and rationals (B.1.b), numeral systems (B.1.c), and number systems and their properties in real analysis (D.1.b).
Independence of the axiom of choice and continuum hypothesis from ZFC and incompleteness results directly address limitations of axiomatic set theory and Gödel's theorem (A.3.b).
Fundamental theorem of arithmetic and prime distribution relate to elementary number theory (E.2.a); prime number theorem and Riemann zeta function connect to analytic number theory (E.2.c).
Dedekind cuts and Cauchy sequences defining the complete ordered field relate to number systems and their properties (D.1.b) and origins of real analysis (D.1.a).
Zero as additive identity relates to fundamental definitions and laws of arithmetic (B.1.a); zero as a placeholder digit enabling positional notation relates to number systems (B.1.c).
Functions as central objects of calculus and analysis relate to functions and differential calculus (D.1.c); functions generalize to polynomials and rational functions in algebra (B.2.c).
Historical survey touching all branches -- set theory, algebra, geometry, analysis, number theory, and topology -- provides developmental context for the entire taxonomy of mathematical branches.
Fractions as part-to-whole ratios with numerator and denominator directly correspond to the theory of divisors and fractions resulting from division (B.1.b).

Solving polynomial, linear, and Diophantine equations is the principal problem of elementary algebra (B.2.d); differential and parametric equations fall under differential equations in analysis (D.3).
Area of polygons and polyhedra relates to the measure of polygons (C.1.b); generalization via Lebesgue measure connects to measure and integral calculus (D.1.d).
Euclid's Elements established axiomatic Euclidean geometry (C.1, C.1.a); his proof of the infinitude of primes connects to elementary number theory (E.2.a).

Quadratic reciprocity and Disquisitiones Arithmeticae in elementary and algebraic number theory (E.2.a, E.2.b); non-Euclidean geometry and Gaussian curvature (C.2, C.6); Gaussian distribution in probability theory (D.6).
Limits define continuity, derivatives, and integrals, foundational to real analysis (D.1, D.1.a); central to functions and differential calculus (D.1.c).
Complex analysis is the theory of analytic functions of complex variables (D.2, D.2.a); complex numbers as homogeneous coordinates in projective geometry (C.3.c).
Decisive contributions to analysis (D.1, D.2), number theory (E.2), and founding of graph theory and topology (F.1).
Reshaped abstract algebra with the modern theory of rings (B.4.d), ideals, fields (B.4.c), and modules; Noetherian chain conditions apply to algebraic structures broadly (B.4).
Deductive argument establishing truth from axioms connects to the axiomatization of the foundations of geometry and results derived from axioms (C.1.a).
Infinite series, convergence theory, power series, Fourier series, and Dirichlet series directly correspond to the theory of series (D.5.a) and Fourier series (D.5.b).
Logarithm as inverse of exponentiation, reducing multiplication to addition, directly matches the formal definition and use of logarithms (B.1.e).
Angles are central to trigonometry (C.4.b) and fundamental geometric figures in Euclidean geometry (C.1).
Trigonometric functions, laws of sines and cosines, and Euler's formula directly correspond to trigonometry within analytic and trigonometric geometry (C.4.b).
Interior angle sums and area formulas relate to the measure of polygons and polyhedra (C.1.b); tilings and triangulation connect to combinatorial geometry (E.1.c).
Simplest polygon with congruence and similarity theorems in Euclidean geometry (C.1.b); fundamental to trigonometric functions and the Pythagorean theorem (C.4.b).
Cantor's multiple infinite cardinalities relate to Cantorian set theory (A.2.b); limit concept and convergence in calculus and analysis (D.1).
Volume of polyhedra relates to the measure of polygons and polyhedra (C.1.b); volume integrals connect to measure and integral calculus (D.1.d).
Encompasses all branches: set theory (A), algebra (B), geometry (C), analysis (D), combinatorics and number theory (E), and topology (F).

Ratio of circumference to diameter in Euclidean geometry (C.1); appears in trigonometric identities (C.4.b); recurrence in Fourier analysis and Gaussian integrals (D.5); connection to Riemann zeta function in analytic number theory (E.2.c).

Kolmogorov's axioms, sample spaces, Bayes' theorem, and interpretations correspond to heuristic introduction to probability (D.6.a), finite dimensional (D.6.b), and infinite dimensional probability spaces (D.6.c).
First systematic treatise on algebra presenting solutions of linear and quadratic equations connects to algebra as extension of arithmetic (B.2.a) and solution of equations (B.2.d).
Fundamental Euclidean geometric figure (C.1); analytic equation in plane analytic geometry (C.4.a); as a conic section and special curve (C.4.e).
Inventor of the Cartesian coordinate system and analytic geometry connecting algebra to geometry (C.4, C.4.a); coordinates and transformation of coordinates (C.4.c).
Variables as symbols representing unspecified mathematical objects are fundamental to algebra as an extension and generalization of arithmetic (B.2.a) and basic algebraic properties (B.2.b).
